title: Six Foundations for Spiritual Battle (Ephesians 6:10-13) || How to Fight in the Dark || David Platt
transcript: Well, if you have a Bible, and I hope you or somebody around you does that, that you can look on with, let me invite you to open with me to Ephesians chapter 6. Feel free to use table of contents if you need to. It's near the back of the Bible. Ephesians chapter 6. And as you're turning there, I want to welcome our NBC family in locations around our city from Moco to Lowden to Arlington to Prince William, as well as others online who are physically unable to be with us today. And in particular, I want to welcome those of you who are visiting with us. Whether you're a follower of Jesus or not, we are sincerely glad and honored that you're here. Want you to know that you are always welcome here. And I also want to in particular welcome younger kids who are in our worship gatherings this summer. While we don't have some of our normal programming at some of our locations, it's good to gather with kids from all ages to worship the one who said, "Let the children come to me." So, it's good to be together with all that that entails. Um, I want to start today by asking you a couple questions and I want to encourage you to really think about the answer to them. So first, how is Satan currently attacking you and others who are close to you? >> Now this question presupposes that Satan is real. >> Yes. >> And that he is currently attacking you. >> Yes. >> And currently attacking others who are close to you. >> Yes. All of which I'm about to show you in the Bible is true. But I want to give you a moment to consider this question knowing there are countless possible answers to it in light of Satan's varied schemes. So, if I could put those schemes or those strategies in general categories, how is Satan most tempting you to sin in your life right now in your thoughts, your desires, your words, your actions? How is Satan most enticing your flesh? Maybe one way to put it, what sins are besetting you? What sins do you find yourself continually struggling with? Statistics would say that lust and pornography would be on that list for a large percentage of people in this gathering. Or maybe it's pride or anger or greed or envy or gossip or any number of sins of omission. So things God is calling you to do but Satan is tempting you to not do or so here's another category. How is Satan attacking you with doubt or discouragement or despair? I was reading first Kings 19 recently where Elijah, this prophet, this man mighty with God was weary of life itself. And I am confident that amidst a gathering this size in all these different places, some of you, maybe many of you are either there or on the verge of being there, weary, wondering how or if you can go on, wondering why this is happening or when this will end. First Kings 19 is a reminder that not one of us is beyond being overwhelmed with disappointment, anxiety, worry, or fear that can lead us to despair of life itself? Or how is Satan attacking your emotional or mental or physical well-being? Or how is Satan attacking some of your closest relationships? Which then leads to the second part of this question. How is Satan attacking others who are close to you? Because he is not content just to come after you. Particularly if he cannot get to you. He will come after those who are closest to you. So how is Satan currently attacking you and others who are close to you? And then to broaden the question, how is Satan currently at work in the world around us? knowing Satan is not just focused on you and me right now, but on the entire world. So, what are some specific ways you see Satan at work in our nation, in other nations, in worldly ideologies and institutions and injustices and idolatry, in various manifestations of evil in the world. And the reason I'm asking you these questions today is to help open your eyes to the spiritual war that we live in in this world. >> So today we're beginning a series that we're calling how to fight in the dark. >> Amen. >> And the plan is for the next eight weeks to explore how all of us live every single day in the middle of spiritual battle. that's raging in us and around us. And we're specifically going to walk through one passage in the Bible, Ephesians 6:10-20, just verse by verse, phrase by phrase to see how to fight this spiritual battle. So, let me read it to you, the whole passage. You can follow along in your Bible or I have it up here on the screen. God says to you and me, starting in Ephesians chapter 6:10, finally, be strong in the Lord and in the strength of his might. Put on the whole armor of God that you may be able to stand against the schemes of the devil. For we do not wrestle against flesh and blood, but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the cosmic powers over this present darkness, against the spiritual forces of evil in the heavenly places. Therefore, take up the whole armor of God, that you may be able to withstand in the evil day, and having done all, to stand firm. Stand therefore, having fastened on the belt of truth, and having put on the breastplate of righteousness, and as shoes for your feet, having put on the readiness given by the gospel of peace. In all circumstances, take up the shield of faith with which you can extinguish all the flaming darts of the evil one. and take the helmet of salvation and the sword of the spirit, which is the word of God, praying at all times in the spirit, with all prayer and supplication. To that end, keep alert with all perseverance, making supplication for all the saints, and also for me, the words may be given to me, and opening my mouth boldly to proclaim the mystery of the gospel, for which I am an ambassador in chains, that I may declare it boldly as I ought to speak. There is so much here. But what I want to do today is show you just a few foundations in the first few verses of this passage that I believe are critical for us to realize and I'm convinced many if not most Christians are completely missing on a daily basis. So, if you're taking notes, I want to show you six truths that you need to know in light of the spiritual battle that is raging in and around you right now. And I I want to encourage you to write these truths down so you can go back to them and look at them later, discuss them with others, whether it's a family or friends or church group. So, here we go. Truth number one, we live in a spiritual world. So, grammatically, this truth is the ground of this entire passage. Like, in just a minute, we'll get to these commands to be strong and stand firm. But notice that these commands exist because of what we read in verse 12. For so, we must be strong, stand firm for because we live in a world that's not just flesh and blood. Yes, it's a physical world and it's a spiritual world filled with rulers and authorities and cosmic powers over darkness and spiritual forces of evil in heavenly places. Therefore, in light of all of that, do these things. But here's the problem. Most of us don't see this world or our everyday lives in it this way. transcript: Good morning and welcome to our service this morning. >> Hello, everyone. Welcome to our online worship service here at McLean Bible Church. I pray now that you will meet us in our homes and meet us on our patios and in our cars. I know we're all social distancing God but you aren't. So Lord, as we go forward and listen to the word this morning, I pray that you will give us receptive hearts and ears to hear as we discuss issues of race and justice, Lord. Give us a deep love for unity and understanding, God. More than anything, God, we want to meet with you this morning and so we look forward to what you will do in Jesus' name, amen. >> So worship with us. >> Let's get ready to lift our voices to the Lord to declare his glory so wherever it is that we are, young and old, let's sing his praises all together. Let's sing this together. ♪♪ >> Church, would you pray with me? Dear Jesus, our hope is in you alone. As a reality, that's the truth and what we hold onto. We have a living hope in you, Jesus. In your resurrection. We have life, Lord God, and we say thank you today. We say thank you, Lord. Thank you so much for your Spirit. Spirit, through your work, you are making us more like Christ day-by-day. And we thank you for that. And we trust that you will continue to work in and through us day-by-day as your church, Lord. We ask that you bring us closer together, one body in Christ. That's what you say we are. We are one body, Lord God, so help us, Lord, by your Spirit to operate just like that. We lack wisdom in how to do that. Aside from your Lord, Lord, your Spirit working in and through us Lord, so we ask you to give us wisdom, do it through your word. We ask and believe that you will do it generously, Lord. Day-by-day, Lord God, help us to be one body, Christ unified, unified in you alone. We trust in you today in and through us, Lord. Help us to do the things that you require of us, Lord. To do justice, to love mercy, to walk humbly with you, our God. It's in Jesus' name that we all pray, amen. >> Boys and girls gather round. Today I have some sweet news I want to share with you. But first a question. How many of you like candy? Oh, yeah. I thought so. Matter of fact, I like it, too, probably too much. And in front of me today I have a big bowl of M & Ms. Look at all that chocolate goodness and look at those amazing colors. And it smells delicious, too. Now, as I was looking at all those colors I thought I bet the boys and girls have a favorite color they like to eat. And did you know as different and as unique as each one of those M & Ms are on the outside, they are exactly the same on the inside. And that made me think of one of my favorite verses I want to read for you now. If you have a Bible look up Psalm 139:14 and allow me to read it to you now. This is the Word of God. I praise you for imwonderfully made. Wonderful are your works. My soul knows it very well. Did you see that word wonderfully? Did you know in the Hebrew we pronounce that word PALA. Think of a dog paw and the law that we don't want to break. A cool word. Did you know that each one of us boys and girls in the history of the world have never had a copy made. When God made you, you are an original. There has never been a boy or girl made just like you. Matter of fact, even twins have differences. I know this from experience. My dad is an identical twin. I can easily tell the difference between him and my uncle. So I find that quite fascinating. As different as each one of us are on the outside, each one of us are the same on the inside. Specifically we are made to be filled with something, too. So think about M & Ms. They are lots of pretty colors and they are all different on the outside but on the inside they were designed to be filled with chocolate and chocolate is really sweet. But did you know that each one of us are designed to be filled with God's love? And that's even sweeter because God's love will last forever. And as I was thinking about M & Ms and God's love I was also thinking M & Ms are easy to share and people love to receive it. But did you know God's love, it's easy to share, too. And so many people need to receive it. And so I have a challenge for you boys and girls today. Think about how you could share God's love with others. And then maybe have your parents help you share on social media. We have this thing called #MBC together. Your parents know what it means. Maybe they could show the world how you are sharing God's love. That's my challenge for you because boys and girls, we have sweet news to share today and I pray that each one of you will have the courage to share it. So until next time, bye-bye. >> Well, welcome to those of you who are watching wherever you are watching from, we're glad to be gathered together virtually around God's Word and if you have been just staying connected with us for a while, you realize you are seeing something different on stage right now. You are seeing not just one long-winded preacher but two long-winded preachers. We will attempt to do something today that I have never done before, that is tag team preach a sermon. Today we will be talking about unity and diversity. We'll be in Psalm 133. So if you have a Bible you can go ahead and turn there. We'll be talking about unity and diversity. But specifically what we believe is necessary, what we believe is going to be required in order for us to experience Christ exalting unity in the midst of the diversity in the church. As David mentioned last Sunday in light of recent events in our country as well as where God has us in our Bible reading plan, we felt compelled to address what the facts show is one of the biggest threats to our unity in the church and that's issues of race and justice. And what we are going to talk about and walk through together will likely bring up all kinds of different emotions and different thoughts. But I want to assure you that our goal in these next few minutes is to address these issues with biblical truth and pastoral sensitivity. Now, even though this is a difficult topic we want this to feel like a family conversation. Just like you often have to have difficult conversations with your family. And speaking of family, we believe that as a church, it's our responsibility to help equip the next generation to understand race and justice from a biblical perspective. But for those of you who are parents, we also realize that you might want to be the one to introduce these topics to your children. So if you're watching with your kids, we would encourage you to either use this time, this message as a way to engage your kids or if you think it's best, feel free to pause now and come back to this message sometime when you are alone. Most of you are aware by now of a situation that has overtaken headlines over the last week and a half. A young black man named Ahmaud Arbery was running through a neighborhood outside Brunswick, Georgia and chased down, shot and killed by two white men. The men were eventually charged with murder and aggravated assault about 2 1/2 months after the incident but only after the video went viral last Tuesday. Now, the point of our time together isn't to evaluate all the details of that particular tragedy. But as many people have said, the local officials didn't bring charges because they saw the video but because we, the American public, saw the video and that actually has been the crux of the public outrage. We're not going to adjudicate what happened in that case. We are praying for the Lord to bring justice as that case continues to unfold. But the reality is that since then, there are new similar tragedies resulting in new headlines. And as expected, those headlines have sparked a very common cycle of deep pain and outrage met by denial and bitter debate. That vicious cycle not only turns in the culture but in our church. You've probably seen that cycle in your conversations on social media, maybe in our church before. And not just when it comes to situations that aggravate black/white tensions in our country but it khurns in a variety of racial justice issues and we want to allow God's Word and his Spirit to guide us and change us as we think about these things. So before we read Psalm 133, would you just take a moment, just take a moment to surrender whatever you are thinking, whatever you are feeling in this moment to God. Just between you and the Lord. Imagine giving those thoughts and feelings to the Lord and would you just invite him through his word to speak to you and invite him to do whatever work he wants to do in your heart. Take a minute between you and God. Father, we are gathered under the authority of your word. And God, we invite you to speak to our hearts and Lord, we know that your word is living and it is active. It is powerful and it always accomplishes everything that you send it forth to accomplish. God, would you accomplish your work in our hearts as we sit under your word? We pray this in Jesus' name, amen. >> With that prayer, let's hear from God's Word. Psalm 133. I will put it on the screen in case you don't have it in front of you. Psalm 133:1 says behold how good and pleasant it is when brothers dwell in unity. It is like the precious oil on the head running down on the beard, on the beard of Aaron, running down on the collar of his robes. It is like the dew of Hermon which falls on the mountains of Zion. For there the Lord has commanded the blessing, life forevermore. Just think about this. That we would be in this text on this week in our Bible reading. That we would read these three simple verses and based on them we want to show us as a church two significant truths about unity in diversity. You might write them down. Here is the first one. According to God's Word, unity in diversity is something that God requires and we desire. Unity in diversity, it is a loaded statement here. We'll unpack it all. It's something God requires and we desire. So let me show that to you specifically in what we just read. Psalm 133 is a song of a sense which means God's people would sing it together as they would travel up to Jerusalem for a feast or a festival. You would have different people, individuals, families from different places, coming together singing this Psalm in unity. So this is a powerful picture of unity in diversity. When we hear brothers dwelling in unity, picture these brothers and sisters from different tribes, from the tribe of Judah over here, from the tribe of Simeon and Asher. Each tribe has its way of doing certain things. It's approach to this or that. They would come together and there was this remarkable unity. They were at home together. They experienced unity even as they expressed diversity. Let me say that one more time. They experienced unity even as they expressed diversity. That's why we're using that language. Unity in diversity. Not unity that denies diversity or ignores diversity, but unity that appreciates and values diversity. Verse 1 here Psalm 133 says unity in diversity like this is good and it is pleasant. So think about that. It is good, it's right, meaning, that's where we get God requires it. It is right and good and it is also pleasant, which means we desire it. Or maybe another way to put that, it's good, which means it is something we ought to pursue. God calls us to pursue this kind of unity. We ought to pursue it and it is pleasant, which means we want to pursue it. We want this kind of unity. God requires it, we desire it. So we read this this week as a church and I know not everybody who is listening in right now is a part of MBC but think about this when it comes to your church family and even us as the broader church. But thinking even specifically about MBC. We have over 100 different countries represented among us. So many different ethnicities with diverse backgrounds and diverse perspectives. Even within our ethnicities not all Caucasians or African-Americans or Asia Americans have the same experiences or think the same way. Here is one of the challenges. As I've talked with some people in our church I've heard from people, particularly from different ethnicities than me who say that while they love MBC, they still oftentimes feel like welcomed guests in this church family. Like Mike and I have talked about this before. So what does that mean when you hear or even say the term welcome to guests or hear that from others, what does that mean? >> Yeah, it's an analogy that came really out of my reflecting on my personal experience. I found that it captures what a lot of people feel in predominantly white churches. Oftentimes people of color are welcomed into the white churches. People and leaders are nice and we're invited to be involved and for the most part it is a good experience. That's not always true by any means. For some people it's a horrible experience. The point is that even when it is a good experience, there is often still this underlying feeling that I'm in the house but it is not my home. It's like a bed and breakfast, right? I get to eat at the table, I can use all the amenities, I even get a bed and a room, but the menu is pre-determined, my pictures aren't on the wall. I can't rerairng the furniture or change the paint color. I am just a welcomed guest. That feeling is more than just a feeling, it is a reality that many people have written about and it has been confirmed most recently in Dr. Korie Edwards research. A Christian sociologist leading a study on diversity in churches and summarizes the research this way. Her words, in short, I propose that interracial churches work -- that is they remain racially integrated to the extent that they are first comfortable places for whites to attend. Now let that sink in for a minute. She says interracial churches, churches like McLean Bible Church and others like it only work to the extent that they are first comfortable places for whites to attend. Not just related to things like cultural preferences or diversity in leadership. The reality that we're welcomed guests. When we often feel like welcomed guests it becomes most clear and most painful when we try to raise issues of justice. I guess I could summarize it this way. For people of color, oftentimes -- not all the time, but oftentimes it feels like our presence is welcomed but not our perspectives or our priorities. >> So I hear that and everything in me just wants to say no, like this is not my house, this is not a house for white people that other people are welcome in like no, this is our house where all of God's people, not just welcome like a guest but you actually feel at home. And in a way that is even greater than any one of us might experience if we were just with people who all look or think like us. So here is what we want to do at this point. Yes, we want to walk through this word together, Mike and myself as pastors, but we also want to pause and hear there some other perspectives in the church. Mike and I asked a few different pastors and their wives from different ethnicities to get together for part one of a multi-part conversation this week about unity in diversity particularly as it relates to race and justice so you can actually find that whole conversation -- it is just part one a little over an hour but we have it out at McLean Bible.org/race. For now, we want you to hear some small samples from different people's perspectives on unique challenges that various ethnicities face in our culture. And there are so many caveats I want to mention here. I mention a couple big ones. One I want to be clear Mike and I are not saying -- these pastors and their wives are not saying they represent everyone in their ethnicity. I just mention even within a certain ethnic group we know there are many different perspectives. That's one big caveat. Another one is we have not included every single ethnic group. When you think about over 100 different countries in our church we would be here a really long time. So it's not every single ethnic group represented. One other thing I would say, we asked these pastors and their wives to feel freedom to share honestly, to be raw in a sense, and one of the reasons we oftentimes don't feel comfortable talking about these things is because many people are afraid of what others will think if we really share how we feel. There is a risk in putting yourself out there in that way. So in sharing these brothers and sisters are taking a risk that I trust we as a church family will honor and listen to and learn from. So with those caveats, we can do a ton more. Let me just start by inviting you to hear from Eric and Jenique. We asked them specifically to describe their thoughts when they heard about Ahmaud Arbery's death. Listen with us. >> When I heard about the death of Ahmaud Arbery feelings of fear yet again came to the surface. It was a reminder for me what I feel like to be a rational thoughts are possible. The three most important people in my life, my husband and my two sons could actually be perceived as threats because of how they look and their lives could be taken away from them. So in the case of Ahmaud Arbery and so many other cases that are similar, these are not stories for me. I take every unjust death of an unarmed black man personal and I have to grieve each one. So today I continue to grieve and I wonder if my brothers and sisters in Christ understand the degree to which that affects me. I grieve as I wonder if my brothers and sisters in Christ understand how that relates to the sanctity of my life of my husband and my two sons. And I grieve the precautions that we take as a family and also the conversations that we will have to have with our sons in the future. >> My wife, how we as a family are processing this. There are a lot of people out here stressed with grief, sadness and anger. If I could give an analogy related to a movie of so many people. We view the events around the death of Ahmaud Arbery many people say in order to deal with these events we need to pause the narrative and reframe it and focus on the facts of this case, we need to focus on these facts disconnected from other racist events in our country's history. However, when I look in the eyes of my mother, or even think about my father or think about my family, we can't do that. This event is connected to a narrative that has gone and is as old as our nation's history. The narrative includes names like Castille and others, and the theme of the sad narrative sadly is this: that this country, including many people that name Jesus Christ as Lord, could care less about the rights of black lives and could care less about their very lives. >> Those are difficult words to process but that's why I love being in such a diverse family because we get to learn from each other's perspectives and this next couple that is going to share their perspective DJ and Jessica are two of my favorite people period. They have been so pivotal in my wife and I's growth as we have thought about and processed these kinds of issues in community. So we asked DJ and Jess to share how they processed racism in our country. >> I'm DJ and my wife Jessica and we've been asked to share how we've processed issues of prejudice and racism in our country. >> I would say for me the answer is I'm still processing. I think it's hugely important that we all approach this with as much humility as possible. And an openness to say there is always more that I can learn. Moving from a position of ignorance to empathy has been a process for me. Initially I was very ignorant because of how I grew up in a fairly non-diverse bubble and then I even moved to a place of willful ignorance because I was so fearful to say the wrong thing or ask the wrong question and then have anger directed at me. It just seemed easier to avoid it all together. And I know that's wrong and through the conviction of the Lord and his grace, he has brought amazing relationships into DJ and my wife, people who have helped us to move from a position of ignorance to a greater understanding and an empathy. Instead of leaving that sin with God and allowing him to forgive it and to forget it, they pick it back up and they carry that burden of guilt where ever they go. Now, one of the things that made my high school track coach go crazy was when boys and girls would look to their left or they would look to their right or even behind them when they were running a race to see who was close to them.
